2605.01011.title: CLEAR: Revealing How Noise and Ambiguity Degrade Reliability in LLMs for Medicine
2605.01011.paper_summary: Medical large language model (LLM) evaluations rely on simplified, exam-style benchmarks that rarely reflect the ambiguity of real-world medical inquiries. We introduce the CLinical Evaluation of Ambiguity and Reliability (CLEAR) framework, which assesses how decision-space presentation, ambiguity, and uncertainty affect LLMs' reasoning on medical benchmarks. CLEAR systematically perturbs (1) the number of plausible answer options, (2) the presence of a ground truth or abstention option, and (3) the semantic framing of answer options. Applying CLEAR on three benchmarks evaluated across 17 LLMs reveals three notable limitations of existing evaluation methods. First, increasing the number of plausible answers degrades a model's ability to identify the correct answer and abstain against incorrect ones. Second, this lack of caution intensifies as the framing of abstention shifts from assertive rejection like "None of the Above" to uncertainty admission like "I don't know" (IDK). Notably, just including IDK in the answer space increases incorrect answer selections. Lastly, we formalize the performance gap between identifying the correct answer and abstaining from incorrect ones as the humility deficit, which worsens with model scale. Our findings reveal limitations in standard medical benchmarks and underscore that scaling alone does not resolve LLM reliability issues.
2605.01011.paper_summary_zh: 醫療大型語言模型 (LLM) 的評估依賴於簡化的考試風格基準，這些基準很少反映現實世界醫療查詢的模糊性。我們介紹了臨床模糊性和可靠性評估 (CLEAR) 框架，該框架評估決策空間的呈現、模糊性和不確定性如何影響 LLM 在醫療基準上的推理。CLEAR 系統性地擾動 (1) 可行答案選項的數量，(2) 真實答案或放棄選項的存在，以及 (3) 答案選項的語義框架。在對 17 個 LLM 進行評估的三個基準上應用 CLEAR 顯示了現有評估方法的三個顯著限制。首先，增加可行答案的數量會降低模型識別正確答案和放棄錯誤答案的能力。其次，隨著放棄的框架從像「以上皆非」這樣的明確拒絕轉變為像「我不知道」(IDK) 這樣的不確定承認，這種缺乏謹慎的情況會加劇。值得注意的是，僅在答案空間中包含 IDK 就會增加錯誤答案的選擇。最後，我們將識別正確答案和放棄錯誤答案之間的表現差距正式化為謙遜缺口，這一缺口隨著模型規模的增大而惡化。我們的發現揭示了標準醫療基準的限制，並強調僅僅擴大規模並不能解決 LLM 的可靠性問題。
2605.01011.author: Kevin H. Guo et.al.
2605.01011.authors: Kevin H. Guo, Chao Yan, Avinash Baidya, Katherine Brown, Xiang Goa, Juming Xiong, Zhijun Yin, Bradley A. Malin
